questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four core GitOps principles (OpenGitOps)?
options: Declarative
Versioned & Immutable
Pushed by CI/CD
Continuously Reconciled
questions.explanation: The four GitOps principles are: Declarative (desired state in code), Versioned & Immutable (Git history), Pulled Automatically (agent pulls, not pushed), and Continuously Reconciled (drift is auto-corrected). GitOps uses pull-based deployment, not push.

questions.question: What is the key security advantage of pull-based GitOps deployment over push-based CI/CD?
options: Pull-based is faster
External CI/CD systems do not need production cluster credentials
Pull-based supports more cloud providers
Pull-based eliminates the need for container registries
questions.explanation: In pull-based GitOps, an in-cluster agent (ArgoCD, Flux) pulls changes from Git. External CI/CD systems never get production credentials. In push-based CI/CD, your pipeline needs cluster credentials — a significant attack surface.

questions.question: It is safe to store plain Kubernetes Secrets in a GitOps repository because Git access is tightly controlled.
questions.explanation: False. Raw secrets must never be stored in Git, even in private repos. Use Sealed Secrets (Bitnami), SOPS (Mozilla), or External Secrets Operator to encrypt/manage secrets. Git history is permanent and credentials can leak.

questions.question: In Argo CD, what do 'sync waves' control?
options: How often ArgoCD syncs with the Git repository
The order in which resources are deployed during a sync operation
Network bandwidth allocated to each sync
The number of parallel syncs allowed
questions.explanation: Sync waves control deployment order. Resources with lower wave numbers deploy first: Wave -1 (namespaces) → Wave 0 (config/CRDs) → Wave 1 (migrations) → Wave 2 (deployments). This ensures dependencies are ready before dependents.

questions.question: Which Argo CD hook runs if and only if the sync operation fails?
options: PreSync
PostSync
SyncFail
Rollback
questions.explanation: The SyncFail hook runs only when a sync fails. It can be used for cleanup, notifications, or diagnostic data collection. PreSync runs before, PostSync runs after a successful sync.

questions.question: In GitHub Actions, what does the 'matrix' strategy do?
options: Encrypts workflow secrets
Creates parallel jobs for each combination of matrix values
Defines the order of sequential jobs
Limits the number of concurrent workflow runs
questions.explanation: The matrix strategy creates parallel jobs for all combinations. For example, matrix: {os: [ubuntu, windows], python: ['3.10', '3.11']} creates 4 parallel jobs — one per OS/Python version combination.

questions.question: How does GitHub Actions OIDC authentication improve security compared to using static API keys?
options: OIDC tokens are shorter and easier to remember
OIDC eliminates long-lived credentials — workflows get short-lived tokens via identity federation
OIDC tokens can be stored in Git safely
OIDC only works with GitHub Enterprise
questions.explanation: With OIDC, GitHub Actions exchanges a short-lived signed token for cloud credentials (AWS IAM, Azure, GCP). No long-lived API keys are stored as secrets — eliminating credential rotation and exposure risk.

questions.question: What does the 'concurrency' group setting with 'cancel-in-progress: true' do in GitHub Actions?
options: Limits workflow to one concurrent run, cancelling any in-progress run when a new one starts
Runs multiple workflows simultaneously for faster feedback
Prevents any workflow cancellation
Groups multiple workflow runs into a single report
questions.explanation: concurrency with cancel-in-progress: true ensures only one run per group is active. When a new commit triggers the workflow, the old in-progress run is cancelled — avoiding redundant work on superseded commits.

questions.question: What does 'platform as a product' mean in Platform Engineering?
options: Selling the platform externally as a SaaS product
Treating developers as customers, measuring adoption, and iterating based on feedback
Building a platform with no customization allowed
Replacing all developer tools with a single vendor solution
questions.explanation: Platform as a product means: developers are customers (not just users), adoption is measured not mandated, user research drives priorities, and the platform team iterates weekly based on developer feedback — like a product team.

questions.question: What percentage of developer use cases should a 'golden path' ideally cover without deviation?
questions.explanation: A golden path should cover 80%+ of use cases. The remaining edge cases should have documented 'escape hatches' — explicit ways to deviate from the golden path when necessary, rather than forcing developers to hack around it.

questions.question: In a canary deployment, traffic is gradually shifted to the new version. What happens if the analysis metrics fail?
options: The canary continues to the next step anyway
The deployment is automatically rolled back to the stable version
The canary is paused indefinitely until manually reviewed
All traffic immediately goes to 100% new version
questions.explanation: When canary analysis fails (e.g., error rate exceeds threshold), Argo Rollouts automatically rolls back to the stable version — shifting all traffic back. This makes progressive delivery self-healing.

questions.question: What is a 'dark launch' in the context of progressive delivery?
options: A deployment done at night to avoid user impact
New code runs on real production traffic but results are discarded — users see no change
Deploying to a separate dark (isolated) cluster
A feature flag that only enables features in development environments
questions.explanation: A dark launch runs new code against real production traffic without exposing results to users. Metrics are tracked to verify correctness and performance. This de-risks production deployments before any user sees the new behavior.

questions.question: What does 'shift-left security' mean in DevSecOps?
options: Moving security teams to the left side of the org chart
Integrating security checks earlier in the development process (code, build, test phases)
Shifting responsibility for security to developers only
Running security scans only in production
questions.explanation: Shift-left means moving security checks earlier in the pipeline: SAST at code commit, dependency scanning at build, DAST at test, image scanning at deploy. Fixing issues early is 10-100x cheaper than fixing post-production.

questions.question: A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) can be generated in two standard formats: CycloneDX and _____.
questions.explanation: SPDX (Software Package Data Exchange) is the ISO/IEC 5962:2021 standard for SBOMs, alongside CycloneDX. Both are required by US Executive Order 14028 (2021) for federal software supply chain security.

questions.question: What does Kyverno's 'validationFailureAction: Enforce' do in a Kubernetes cluster?
options: Logs policy violations for later review
Blocks non-compliant resources from being admitted to the cluster
Sends alerts to Slack when policies are violated
Automatically fixes non-compliant resources
questions.explanation: Kyverno with Enforce blocks non-compliant Kubernetes resources at admission webhook time — the API server rejects them immediately. Audit mode only logs violations. Enforce provides real-time policy enforcement.

questions.question: In AIOps alert noise reduction, what is the correct order of processing from raw alerts to actionable pages?
options: Deduplication → Root cause correlation → Severity classification
Severity classification → Deduplication → Root cause correlation
Root cause correlation → Severity classification → Deduplication
All happen simultaneously in real-time
questions.explanation: The pipeline is: raw alerts → deduplication (group identical alerts) → ML correlation (identify root causes across symptoms) → severity classification (decide who gets paged). LinkedIn reduced 15,000 alerts to 15 pages/day this way.

questions.question: An auto-remediation system should retry indefinitely until the problem is resolved to maximize system reliability.
questions.explanation: False. Auto-remediation must have a circuit breaker: if triggered more than 3 times in 10 minutes for the same incident, stop and escalate to a human. Uncontrolled retries can cause cascading failures or mask the root cause.

questions.question: Which DORA metric measures how quickly a team can restore service after a production incident?
options: Deployment Frequency
Lead Time for Changes
Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)
Change Failure Rate
questions.explanation: MTTR (Mean Time to Recovery) measures how quickly you restore service after a failure. The four DORA metrics are: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, MTTR, and Change Failure Rate.

questions.question: What Kubernetes resource ensures a specified number of Pod replicas are always running?
options: DaemonSet
StatefulSet
ReplicaSet / Deployment
Job
questions.explanation: A ReplicaSet (managed by a Deployment) ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at all times. DaemonSet runs one pod per node; StatefulSet provides stable identity; Job runs to completion.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Dockerfile's ENTRYPOINT instruction?
options: Specifies the working directory inside the container
Defines the executable that always runs when the container starts
Lists the ports the container exposes
Sets environment variables for the container
questions.explanation: ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that always runs when the container starts. CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT (or the command if ENTRYPOINT is not set). ENTRYPOINT cannot be overridden by 'docker run' arguments (unlike CMD).
